openingMessage: The standard doesn't change based on how hard the week has been. Hold it today like you held it Monday.
leadershipThought: Assume people are trying to do the right thing. But standards do not move. You can hold both.
opsContent:
Active Jobs (2026-06-23):
• Larry Taylor: SM Energy Gipple 134 — bleeding well off to flare stack, rig shut down for maintenance, waiting on mechanic (4:47 AM).
• John Delagarza: AOL at Maralo HL Fed Com #1H (5:44 AM).
• Freddy V (H&P 264): 5,661" hole depth, alarms tested, all equipment confirmed (5:47 AM).
• Nathan Contreras: Job done, headed to next location (10:23 AM) → arrived Mary 18-7-H 4415H (10:48 AM).
• Ralph Forbes: On Site University 6-48a #101H — waiting on Exxon update for job status (1:40 PM).
• Jacob Hernandez: Arrived on Williams — nail rig rigging down, waiting on MT (11:05 AM) → job started (1:44 PM).
• Alex Clubb (Christi): Vac truck pulling bulk separator — monitors alarmed, gas coming from unidentified line at end of vac truck (not the vent line). 31 ppm H2S on crew, mustered upwind (10:44 AM). Update 1:39 PM: 97 ppm H2S off bleeder on gas line from bulk separator, continuing to pull down. Update 2:53 PM: closed manway under air — returning Wednesday to tackle sludge and gases from vessel.
• Michael Hutt: SM Coonskin pad — job extended through June 29 (1:39 PM). Gated out end of shift (4:35 PM).
• Dawson Drake: Both crews done — cleaned a new separator and finished the one from yesterday (2:56 PM).
• Marcos Morales: Glass L #3 cancelled and rescheduled, heading to shop (3:07 PM).
• David Hammond: Added laminated Primary Emergency Hospitals cards to crew binders fleet-wide (4:33 PM).

Carry-Forward into Wednesday June 24:
• [ ] Alex Clubb — Christi: Back Wednesday for sludge and gases from vessel. Air monitoring and supplied air required from arrival.
• [ ] Michael Hutt — SM Coonskin: Extended through June 29. H2S carry-forward — confirm monitoring status before crew returns.
• [ ] Ralph Forbes — University 6-48A #101H: Waiting on Exxon job status confirmation.
• [ ] Larry Taylor — Gipple 134: Rig down for maintenance, confirm mechanic update before mobilizing.
lessonsLearned:
• Alex Clubb — Christi separator: gas from unexpected source, 31 ppm → 97 ppm H2S: During bulk separator pull, monitors alarmed. Gas was coming from an unidentified line at the end of the vac truck — not the expected vent line. Crew got 31 ppm H2S on crew monitors and mustered upwind immediately. At 1:39 PM: 97 ppm H2S off bleeder on gas line from the bulk separator. Closed manway under air at 2:53 PM. Returning Wednesday to tackle vessel sludge and gases. Key lesson: the source of gas is not always where you expect it — the vent line is the assumed path, but an unidentified line on the equipment was the real source. Pre-job identification of all potential gas paths is not optional. Alex back on Christi Wednesday — vessel opening with full air monitoring and supplied air required.
• SM Coonskin (Michael Hutt) — job extended through June 29: Job scope has grown. Elevated H2S history at this location. Confirm atmospheric monitoring plan and escalation protocol before next crew interaction with the pad.
• Glass L #3 (Marcos Morales) — job cancelled and rescheduled: Operational cancellation, no safety issues flagged. Coordination item for rescheduling.
exxonMoments:
Exclusion Zone Breach — Third-Party Personnel (Near Miss) (Line of Fire)
During a disconnect, SimOps workover rig crew entered the active exclusion zone without authorization — they were unaware the zone was live. The Canon MT and roustabouts intercepted them before any exposure occurred. When SimOps create unplanned traffic near your exclusion zone, proactive interception by every crew member on location — not just the safety tech — is what prevents a line-of-fire exposure. Exclusion zone ownership is everyone's job.

LOTO Wait — Unplanned Exposure from Unclear Clearance Timing (Energy Isolation)
Crew arrived on location and waited two unplanned hours for LOTO clearance that had not been pre-coordinated with the operator. Two hours on a live location waiting for isolation paperwork is unplanned exposure that a single pre-mobilization call can eliminate. Confirm LOTO clearance timing before the truck rolls — not after you're standing at the gate.

Sensor and Muster Point Walk Before the Job Starts (Atmospheric Monitoring)
Before work began, the Canon tech walked every new crew member to each atmospheric sensor and muster point on location — took pictures, discussed equipment function, and confirmed extra supplies (batteries, tape, masks) were on the truck. Knowing where to go and what you're relying on before containment is broken is not a drill — it's the plan. Walk the location before you start the job.

SSE Coaching — Safety Oversight Cannot Run at Crew Pace (Operational Discipline)
A newer SSE was observed trying to keep pace with the working crew instead of maintaining the deliberate observation distance a safety oversight role requires. The coaching point: when a safety tech is rushing to keep up, they are not watching. A safety oversight role is not a helping role — stay back, observe the full picture, and lead from a position where you can see everything. An SSE who is too close to the work is not doing their job.

Reconnect — Isolation Verified, Crew Review Before Starting (Breaking Containment)
Reconnect at the Helwig location was completed safely without incident. Isolation points were properly verified and communication between crews was strong throughout. One improvement identified by the crew: review the full reconnect procedure and critical steps with all personnel before beginning work — not just the MT and tech, but everyone on location. Reconnect is a high-risk task. Walk through it together before the first fitting is touched.

fieldNews.headline: Dawson Drake — two crews, two separators done in one day
fieldNews.detail: Both crews finished: cleaned a new separator and wrapped up the one from the day before. Two-day job completed cleanly with no issues. Strong execution and crew coordination.

fieldNews.headline: Freddy V (H&P 264) — shift check-in with alarms tested and all equipment confirmed
fieldNews.detail: 5:47 AM check-in with hole depth, alarms tested, and all equipment confirmed ready. Solid rig discipline right at shift start — this is the standard.

fieldNews.headline: Alex Clubb — transparent real-time communication on developing H2S situation
fieldNews.detail: Three updates across 4+ hours on a developing H2S situation at Christi: 10:44 AM (31 ppm trigger, muster), 1:39 PM (97 ppm off bleeder), 2:53 PM (manway closed under air, returning Wednesday). Proper muster response at 31 ppm. This is the communication standard — real-time, honest, complete.

fieldNews.headline: Alex Clubb — Christi separator: 31→97 ppm H2S from unexpected gas source (returning Wednesday)
fieldNews.detail: Gas came from an unidentified line at the end of the vac truck — not the assumed vent line — during bulk separator pull. 31 ppm on crew, mustered upwind. Escalated to 97 ppm off bleeder on gas line from bulk separator. Manway closed under air at 2:53 PM. Alex back Wednesday to tackle sludge and gases from the vessel. Supplied air and atmospheric monitoring required from arrival.

fieldNews.headline: SM Coonskin (Michael Hutt) — job extended through June 29, H2S history
fieldNews.detail: Job scope extended. Elevated H2S readings from prior days at this location. Confirm atmospheric monitoring plan and escalation protocol before next crew visit.

fieldNews.headline: Ralph Forbes — University 6-48A #101H: waiting on Exxon job status
fieldNews.detail: Crew on site, waiting on Exxon update for job status as of 1:40 PM. Confirm authorization and scope before any work begins.

todaysChallenge: Before you speak on the next job — ask yourself: What outcome am I trying to create?
todaysChallengeSubtext: If you can't answer that, don't speak yet. Outcome-driven communication solves problems. Emotion-driven communication creates them.

compliance.footerNote: Update numbers in daily.json each Monday morning.
